title: Create several models by splitting the qMatrix and/or person.groups — splitModels
code: splitModels ( qMatrix = NULL , person.groups = NULL ,      split = c ( "qMatrix" , "person.groups" ) , add = NULL , cross = NULL ,      all.persons = TRUE , all.persons.lab = "all" ,      person.split.depth = 0:length(person.groups[,-1,drop=FALSE]) ,      full.model.names = TRUE , model.name.elements = c ( "dim" , "group" , "cross" ) ,      include.var.name = FALSE , env = FALSE , nCores=NULL , mcPackage = c("future", "parallel"),     GBcore=NULL , verbose = TRUE )

title: Create several models by splitting the qMatrix and/or person.groups — splitModels
text: qMatrix argument defineModel: Optional: named data frame indicating items grouped dimensions. first column contains names items named item. columns contain dimension definitions named respective dimension names. positive value (e.g., 1 2 1.4) indicates loading weight item loads dimension, value 0 indicates respective item load dimension. q matrix specified user, unidimensional structure assumed. person.groups data.frame, first row must person ID, columns contain group categories, e.g. data.frame ( "id" = 1:10 , "sex" = sample ( c ( "male" , "female" ) , 10 , replace = TRUE ) ) split character, possible values consequences: NULL: qMatrix person.groups split, one model original qMatrix persons set "qMatrix": qMatrix split single dimensions, number created models equals number dimensions "person.groups": person.groups split single groups, number created models equals number combinations groups (least one person) c("qMatrix","person.groups"): default, qMatrix person.groups split single dimensions single groups crossed, number created models equals number dimension multiplied number combinations groups add list elements single values, names elements arguments defineModel, elements value passed running defineModel; elements add used models; e.g. list ( "software" = "conquest" , "nodes" = 15 ), means models estimated "conquest" 15 nodes cross list elements several values, names elements arguments defineModel, elements value passed running defineModel; elements cross crossed models; e.g. list ( "software" = c("conquest","tam") , "nodes" = c(15,30) ), now models set run "conquest" "tam", 15 30 nodes .persons logical (default: TRUE), group variable person.groups "" category included .persons.lab character, name "" category person.split.depth integer, depth group splits, 0: global persons included, 1: groups variables included, 2: groups pairs variables included, n: groups n variables included. Can vector one argument, e.g. 3 variables, full number splits (also default) can obtained c(0,1,2,3); creates model persons (0), groups variables (1), groups pairs variables (2), groups combining 3 variables (3). Using person.split.depth usually makes sense .persons=TRUE; .persons=FALSE depth equals number variables (another depth set, splits performed). full.model.names logical (default: TRUE), model names derived model.name.elements; FALSE models numbered ascending order model.name.elements character, elements model names built , possible values: "dim" , "group" , "add" , "cross"; default: c ( "dim" , "group" , "cross" ) , means model names include name dimension(s) , group(s) , parameter values crossed include.var.name logical (default: FALSE), include name variable building model names; e.g. (FALSE) "science__sex.female__conquest" , (TRUE) "dim.science__group.sex.female__software.conquest" env logical (default: FALSE) (FALSE) returns list two elements: data.frame model information (model overview), list model specifications (TRUE)  returns list two elements: data.frame model information (model overview), list environments model specifications set objects intended subsequent use defineModel use env=FALSE nCores integer (default: NULL), number cores use subsequent data preparation, model estimation results compilation mcPackage package used local host definition multicore processing? R version < 3.4, "parallel" recommended. R version >= 3.4, "future" recommended. GBcore numeric (default: NULL), maximum RAM usage per core giga bytes verbose logical (default: TRUE), print progress

title: Create several models by splitting the qMatrix and/or person.groups — splitModels
text: depending env either: (env=FALSE) returns list two elements: data.frame model information (model overview), list model specifications (env=TRUE)  returns list two elements: data.frame model information (model overview), list environments model specifications set objects intended subsequent use defineModel use env=FALSE

title: Create several models by splitting the qMatrix and/or person.groups — splitModels
text: Martin Hecht

title: Create several models by splitting the qMatrix and/or person.groups — splitModels
code: # see also examples in 'defineModel' # example qMatrix qMatrix <- data.frame ( "item" = 1:4 , "science" = c(1,1,0,0) ,            "math" = c(0,0,1,1) , stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # example person.groups person.groups <- data.frame ( "person" = 1:4 , "state" = rep(c("Berlin","Bavaria"),2) ,                  "sex" = c(rep("female",2),rep("male",2)) , stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Example 1: one 2-dimensional model with all persons (no split) m01 <- splitModels ( qMatrix=qMatrix, person.groups=person.groups, split=NULL ) #> -------------------------------- #> splitModels: generating 1 models #> . #> see <returned>$models #> number of cores: 1 #> -------------------------------- m01$models #>   model.no                      model.name                    model.subpath          dim Ndim             group Ngroup #> 1        1 science_math__state.all_sex.all ./science_math/state.all_sex.all science_math    2 state.all_sex.all      1  # Example 2: split qMatrix to create two unidimensional models, each with all persons m02 <- splitModels ( qMatrix=qMatrix, person.groups=person.groups, split=c("qMatrix") ) #> -------------------------------- #> splitModels: generating 2 models #> .. #> see <returned>$models #> number of cores: 2 #> -------------------------------- m02$models #>   model.no                 model.name               model.subpath     dim Ndim             group Ngroup #> 1        1 science__state.all_sex.all ./science/state.all_sex.all science    1 state.all_sex.all      1 #> 2        2    math__state.all_sex.all    ./math/state.all_sex.all    math    1 state.all_sex.all      1  # Example 3: split person.groups to create 2-dimensional models, each with a # subgroup of persons m03 <- splitModels ( qMatrix=qMatrix, person.groups=person.groups,        split=c("person.groups") ) #> -------------------------------- #> splitModels: generating 9 models #> ......... #> see <returned>$models #> number of cores: 9 #> -------------------------------- m03$models #>   model.no                             model.name                           model.subpath          dim Ndim                    group Ngroup #> 1        1  science_math__state.Berlin_sex.female  ./science_math/state.Berlin_sex.female science_math    2  state.Berlin_sex.female      1 #> 2        2    science_math__state.Berlin_sex.male    ./science_math/state.Berlin_sex.male science_math    2    state.Berlin_sex.male      1 #> 3        3     science_math__state.Berlin_sex.all     ./science_math/state.Berlin_sex.all science_math    2     state.Berlin_sex.all      1 #> 4        4 science_math__state.Bavaria_sex.female ./science_math/state.Bavaria_sex.female science_math    2 state.Bavaria_sex.female      1 #> 5        5   science_math__state.Bavaria_sex.male   ./science_math/state.Bavaria_sex.male science_math    2   state.Bavaria_sex.male      1 #> 6        6    science_math__state.Bavaria_sex.all    ./science_math/state.Bavaria_sex.all science_math    2    state.Bavaria_sex.all      1 #> 7        7     science_math__state.all_sex.female     ./science_math/state.all_sex.female science_math    2     state.all_sex.female      1 #> 8        8       science_math__state.all_sex.male       ./science_math/state.all_sex.male science_math    2       state.all_sex.male      1 #> 9        9        science_math__state.all_sex.all        ./science_math/state.all_sex.all science_math    2        state.all_sex.all      1  # Example 4: split both qMatrix and person.groups to create unidimensional # models for all subgroups m04 <- splitModels ( qMatrix=qMatrix, person.groups=person.groups,        split=c("qMatrix","person.groups") ) #> --------------------------------- #> splitModels: generating 18 models #> .................. #> see <returned>$models #> number of cores: 18 #> --------------------------------- m04$models #>    model.no                        model.name                      model.subpath     dim Ndim                    group Ngroup #> 1         1  science__state.Berlin_sex.female  ./science/state.Berlin_sex.female science    1  state.Berlin_sex.female      1 #> 2         2    science__state.Berlin_sex.male    ./science/state.Berlin_sex.male science    1    state.Berlin_sex.male      1 #> 3         3     science__state.Berlin_sex.all     ./science/state.Berlin_sex.all science    1     state.Berlin_sex.all      1 #> 4         4 science__state.Bavaria_sex.female ./science/state.Bavaria_sex.female science    1 state.Bavaria_sex.female      1 #> 5         5   science__state.Bavaria_sex.male   ./science/state.Bavaria_sex.male science    1   state.Bavaria_sex.male      1 #> 6         6    science__state.Bavaria_sex.all    ./science/state.Bavaria_sex.all science    1    state.Bavaria_sex.all      1 #> 7         7     science__state.all_sex.female     ./science/state.all_sex.female science    1     state.all_sex.female      1 #> 8         8       science__state.all_sex.male       ./science/state.all_sex.male science    1       state.all_sex.male      1 #> 9         9        science__state.all_sex.all        ./science/state.all_sex.all science    1        state.all_sex.all      1 #> 10       10     math__state.Berlin_sex.female     ./math/state.Berlin_sex.female    math    1  state.Berlin_sex.female      1 #> 11       11       math__state.Berlin_sex.male       ./math/state.Berlin_sex.male    math    1    state.Berlin_sex.male      1 #> 12       12        math__state.Berlin_sex.all        ./math/state.Berlin_sex.all    math what: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
text: Function uses output equat1pl provide two data.frames, one item parameters bista metric one person parameter (PVs) bista metric.

title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
code: transformToBista ( equatingList, refPop, cuts, weights = NULL, defaultM = 500,                     defaultSD = 100, q3bound= .2, roman = FALSE, vera = TRUE,                    idVarName = NULL, years=NULL)

title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
text: equatingList object returned equat1pl. refPop Optional: Data frame least three columns. First column indicates domain name. Note name must match domain names output getResults. Second column contains mean reference population. Third column contains standard deviation reference population. Fourth column optionally contains transformed mean Bista metric reference population. Fifth column optionally contains transformed standard deviation Bista metric reference population. fourth fifth columns missing, values defaulted 500/100. refPop specified, mean SD computed data, optionally using weights (weights argument specified). cuts named list cut scores. Names list must match domain names output getResults. element list named list one two elements—cut scores (ascending order) (optionally) labels stages. first element (cut scores) must named values. second element (labels stages) must named labels. See examples defineModel details. weights Optional: data.frame two columns, first column person identifier, second columns individual caseweight. Necessary transformation linking error (ordered) factors /descriptives reference population computed directly data. See examples defineModel details. defaultM Mean reference population “bista” metric. defaultSD Standard deviation reference population “bista” metric. q3bound Define absolute boundary Q3 values captured item parameter list according guidelines “Vergleichsarbeiten”. roman Logical: Use roman numbers competence level column shortened item parameter table dedicated “Vergleichsarbeiten”? vera Logical: Prepare item parameter list according guidelines “Vergleichsarbeiten”? idVarName Optional: character vector individual student id. provide compatibility older package versions. Specification argument necessary function gives error. years Optional: numeric vector two elements, indicating years assessment. necessary additional linking error object created.

title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
text: list three data frames: first one contains original transformed item parameters competence levels. second one contains original transformed person parameters competence levels. third one contains transformation information.

title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
text: Sebastian Weirich

title: Transformation of item and person parameters to the Bista metric. — transformToBista
